LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD only defines static variables, source entries with checksums, and functions (`prepare`, `package`) that are **not** executed during `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` or `makepkg --printsrcinfo`. The top-level scope contains no command substitutions, `eval`, or any obfuscated code. The only source download is from the official upstream GitHub repository (`https://github.com/aramg/droidcam`), and both source entries have pinned SHA-512 checksums. There is no `pkgver()` function to execute. No data exfiltration, unexpected network requests, or backdoor attempts are present in the sourced portion of this PKGBUILD.
</details>
<summary>No malicious code in top-level scope; sources pinned and benign.</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- No malicious code in top-level scope; sources pinned and benign.[/]

Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines standard source entries: a local `dkms.conf` file and a zip archive downloaded from the project's own upstream GitHub repository (`https://github.com/aramg/droidcam`). The URL uses the `https` protocol, which is secure, and the archive corresponds directly to the declared version `v${pkgver}`. The `sha512sums` array contains valid checksums for both sources, ensuring integrity verification. There is no command substitution, obfuscation, or executable code in the global scope or in `pkgver()` (which is absent and thus defaults to the statically assigned `pkgver=1.3`). The top-level code consists only of safe variable assignments and array definitions. Running `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` will safely fetch and verify the sources without executing any potentially harmful code.
</details>
<summary>
Safe source fetching and no malicious top-level code
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Safe source fetching and no malicious top-level code[/]

Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD fetches two sources: a local `dkms.conf` file and a zip archive from the package's own upstream GitHub repository (`https://github.com/aramg/droidcam`), which matches the declared `url`. Both sources have sha512 checksums provided (non-SKIP), which is good practice for static archives.

There is no top-level code execution, no command substitutions outside of function bodies, and no `pkgver()` function. The `source` array contains only standard HTTP(S) downloads from the legitimate upstream. Nothing in the global scope or sourcing of this PKGBUILD would execute during `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` beyond fetching those two sources.

The `prepare()` and `package()` functions contain only routine packaging operations and are out of scope for this narrow gate.
</details>
<summary>
Standard PKGBUILD fetching sources from legitimate upstream only.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Standard PKGBUILD fetching sources from legitimate upstream only.[/]

data.reasoning: Both audits failed at source download stage (makepkg --nobuild error), preventing examination of critical files. PKGBUILD appears safe but source code remains unaudited.
coverage_issues: Install hooks and build scripts inaccessible
false_negatives: Potential risks in unaudited source code: network/filesystem access in kernel module
Unaudited DKMS install hooks that execute during package installation
Potential malicious code in droidcam driver implementation
learnings: Improve source retrieval robustness: implement retries, mirror fallbacks
Prioritize critical files first: PKGBUILD → source archives → install scripts
Enhance error handling: flag source download failures as critical coverage gaps
Add pre-audit dependency checks to prevent download failures
